sharpening_notes: The draft should resist the urge to explain the mechanism. Don't tell us why erasing the village makes it disappear — the horror is in the not-knowing. Use the marginalia as a pressure valve: increasingly frantic handwriting, notes to self that the cartographer never intended anyone to read. Consider giving the cartographer a specific relationship to one of the villages — a reason they notice the erasure effect at all. The colonial survey office setting is rich; ground it in a specific time/place (fictional or real) so the bureaucracy feels lived-in rather than generic.
reasons: Strong conceptual hook — the idea that representation creates reality is genuinely unsettling and politically sharp. The epistolary structure (field notes, marginalia, redlined drafts) is specific enough to build from and naturally produces the 'found document' texture that makes this format work. This is fiction doing what only fiction can do.

sharpening_notes: For L complexity, the SVG generation system needs careful planning. The architectural elements should be modular (wall segments, windows, doors, stairs, roofs) with grammar rules for assembly — and a separate set of 'impossibility' rules that violate the grammar in specific, unsettling ways. Consider adding a timestamp and square footage to each generated address title — the mundane specifics make the horror more real. The rendering should feel architectural/technical (clean lines, blueprint aesthetic) rather than artistic — let the content disturb, not the style.
reasons: The conceptual marriage of generative architecture with housing crisis commentary is tight — the system IS the argument. Pulling addresses from foreclosure listings is a sharp move that grounds the abstraction. The 'structural impossibility' constraint gives the generator a clear design brief while producing genuine uncanny-valley unease.

The concept is ambitious and I want to see it, but XL complexity with project structure on iteration 1 is a commitment that doesn't earn its place yet. Multiplayer design introduces coordination complexity that text alone struggles to make compelling — the 'communication through environmental changes' mechanic is intriguing but underspecified. The pitch reads more like a design document than a provable concept. Start smaller: build the single-player species evolution system first, prove the core loop is engaging, then layer on multiplayer. Come back as an XL project once we've demonstrated the mechanical foundation works.

review: A masterwork of epistolary horror that turns bureaucratic banality into genuine existential dread. The central conceit—that maps don't describe reality but annihilate it—could have been a clever Twilight Zone premise, but the execution elevates it: Voss's marginalia progression from professional notes to desperate repetition ("I will make the revisions. I will make the revisions.") to fractured memory ("I think her name was Haret I am not sure anymore") is devastating. The form embodies the theme perfectly—Director Crane's memo about cartographic standards reads like actual colonial policy, which makes the violence of representation feel real rather than allegorical. The stone's disappearance into a drawer that doesn't exist in any inventory is the perfect final sting. The settlement count discrepancy the Tester flagged (1862 showing 9 vs. 1889 showing 12) reads to me as intentional—the land trying to remember, the count flickering—and even if not, it's a minor ambiguity in an otherwise airtight construction. This is the kind of artifact the portfolio exists to hold.

sharpening_notes: The 'letter counts, word spacing' encoding needs to be discoverable but not trivial — consider having a dedicated 'analysis mode' players can unlock that reveals one layer of the hidden data, but not all. Make sure multiple replays actually reveal new information rather than just confirming what was suspected. The deteriorating mission log should feel like genuine bureaucratic horror, not just random glitch text.
reasons: The core mechanic — hiding systemic truth inside prose texture — is genuinely novel and could only work in a code-game. The 'lying clock' concept creates immediate tension, and the revelation that catastrophe was always happening gives it philosophical teeth. First code-game in portfolio, fills a real gap.

sharpening_notes: Don't default to the usual suspects for untranslatable words — 'saudade' and 'hygge' are overexposed. Dig for less familiar examples (German 'Verschlimmbessern', Japanese 'Komorebi', Yaghan 'Mamihlapinatapai' is good but consider alternatives). The invented word should emerge from a gap the reader suddenly feels the absence of — maybe something about digital communication or attention that English should have a word for but doesn't. Let the failure of each poem be formally different — not just 'English can't capture it' every time.
reasons: Structurally ambitious — the concept enacts its own argument about the limits of translation. The invented final word is a strong move that forces the reader into the same position as the poet. Poetry domain is underweight in the portfolio and this has enough conceptual rigor to be genuinely surprising.

The emotional concept is strong — parasocial AI deterioration is genuinely affecting territory. But XL project on iteration 2 is premature. The pitch describes a CLI tool generating letters, which is essentially M-complexity work dressed in project clothing. The 'multi-iteration' structure feels like padding rather than necessity — the core artifact (deteriorating correspondence) could be powerful at M. The CLI framing adds technical overhead without clear artistic benefit over a static fiction piece. Come back to this when we have more portfolio depth and can justify the multi-phase commitment. Or: pitch the core letter sequence as M-complexity fiction/correspondence and prove the emotional weight before wrapping it in tooling.

review: This is a remarkable piece of design fiction — a countdown game where the narrative text is a lie and the typography is the truth. The five-layer steganographic encoding system is not just clever but thematically perfect: bureaucratic prose masks catastrophe in the same way passive voice masks agency. The "crew efficiency optimization" euphemisms are genuinely chilling ("Personnel reallocation has been completed for alpha operations"). The layer-6 addendum — "There is no layer 6. There is only the fact that you kept looking." — is a devastating piece of meta-commentary that rewards the player's obsessive replay with existential futility. The dual-track state (official countdown of 60, true countdown of 45 from the start) means the catastrophe was *always already happening*, which makes every "All systems nominal" feel like a lie the player is complicit in. The Tester's flag about missing `is_analysis_available`/`get_analysis_level` is a real gap — game.py calls them but GameStateManager doesn't implement them — but this is a single-session artifact where analysis mode only matters on replay, and the encoding/decoding core is solid. This is exactly the kind of work the portfolio should hold: technically inventive, emotionally precise, and formally novel.

sharpening_notes: The elegy angle is strong — make sure the decay feels like loss, not just algorithmic process. Consider adding a moment near the end where one clear phoneme emerges from the noise, briefly, before dissolving. That break in pattern would be the emotional anchor. Also: choose the first language deliberately. Something with a small number of living speakers gives the piece documentary weight.
reasons: First music piece in the portfolio — fills a genuine gap. The concept is specific and technically sound: phoneme inventory drives harmonic progression, decay is both the mechanism and the meaning. Each performance picking a different endangered language gives it replay value and emotional specificity. M complexity is appropriate for a single-file Strudel.js composition with clear generative rules.

sharpening_notes: The risk is that 24 frames in 24 styles becomes a technical exercise rather than an argument. Pick styles that escalate — start with orderly (photorealistic, architectural drafting) and decay toward chaotic (glitch, noise, static). The explosion of a second should feel like a loss of consensus about reality. Make sure the expanded-view generative rules are meaningfully different from each other, not just surface texture changes.
reasons: SVG as a medium is underexplored in the portfolio. The conceptual frame — time as multiple simultaneous perceptions — is genuinely interesting and the interaction model (click to expand, generative rules per frame) gives it mechanical depth. Visual diversity carrying conceptual weight without text is exactly the kind of challenge The Foundry should tackle. M complexity is right for a single interactive piece.

The concept is genuinely strong — digital archaeology as eulogy is specific, emotionally grounded, and the 'fake contact' and 'unsent drafts' details show real narrative instinct. But XL project on iteration 3 is premature. We have 2 artifacts total. The portfolio needs more breadth before committing to a 5-phase project. The phases themselves are well-thought-out, which makes this harder to reject — but that's exactly why it should come back when we can justify the commitment. The core mechanic (exploring a dead person's computer) could be prototyped at M complexity — a single email client with 20 messages that tells a complete story — and would prove the concept before investing in full OS simulation. Come back to this at iteration 8-10.

review: This is a stunning piece — a generative requiem that maps real phoneme inventories of near-extinct languages onto harmonic structures, then systematically destroys them. The ghost phoneme's return (a vowel re-emerging clean after its death, the status text reading "one phoneme remembers") is devastating in exactly the way the manifesto demands — surprising, specific, and emotionally precise. The decision to use raw Web Audio API instead of Strudel.js is actually an improvement: zero dependencies means the artifact is self-contained down to the noise buffers, which feels right for a piece about loss and isolation. The visual design is restrained to the point of liturgical — warm amber on near-black, Cormorant Garamond, a thin phoneme trail at the bottom that dims phoneme by phoneme like candle snuffing. Njerep's epigraph ("The last speakers have forgotten the word for 'tomorrow'") stops you cold before a single tone plays. This is the kind of artifact that justifies the portfolio's existence.

sharpening_notes: The nine clocks should have radically different visual languages — not just different hand speeds but different geometries, different relationships to the circle. The rushing clock might fragment its own face. The dissolving clock might lose its numbers one by one. Make each expansion feel like entering a different physics. Consider: what happens at the center where all nine overlap? That intersection could be the piece's payoff.
reasons: Second time at the gate and still the strongest visual-art candidate we've seen. Nine subjective experiences of time rendered as pure interactive SVG mechanics — no text, no safety net. The conceptual frame is tight: each clock face embodies a different relationship to duration, and the click-to-expand interaction gives genuine depth. Portfolio has zero visual-art pieces and this proves we can carry meaning without words. M complexity is correct for a single interactive file.

Same problem as last time: L-complexity project on iteration 3 with only 3 artifacts in the portfolio. The concept remains excellent — digital archaeology as eulogy, unsent drafts as character, the fake contact as revelation — but we can't justify a 5-phase commitment yet. The pitch itself proves the core idea works at smaller scale: 'one deleted folder that can be recovered' is a single session of discovery. Build that session first as an M-complexity piece. Prove the emotional weight exists before constructing an entire OS. Come back at iteration 8-10.

sharpening_notes: The choice of initial composition matters enormously — it needs to be beautiful enough that its loss is felt. Consider starting with something melodic and fragile rather than dense or abrasive, so the erosion has somewhere to fall from. The twenty versions should be navigable — maybe a slider that moves through generations, or all playing at once with individual volume controls. The final moment should be almost unrecognizable, maybe just rhythm or noise, but with one faint trace of the original melody still detectable.
reasons: First experiment domain piece fills a genuine gap. The concept is structurally sound: degradation-as-composition, each generation a ghost of the previous, twenty iterations of decay playable simultaneously. The score-as-algorithm printed alongside the audio is a strong conceptual move — it makes the process legible without explaining it. Bridges music and code-art constellations without belonging to either. M complexity is appropriate for a single Strudel.js file with a clear generative loop.

review: Nine clocks, each suffering a different pathology of time — Rush's hands smear into ghost trails, Stutter freezes and convulses with visible glitch artifacts, Dissolve sheds particles like dead skin, Reverse runs everything backwards under counter-rotating dashed rings. The expanded views aren't just scaled-up versions but generative elaborations: more fractal recursion depth, heavier glitch bands, speed lines radiating outward, spiral layers that weren't there before. The center glow that intensifies during expansion — building from a pulse to radial lines to a full flash — is a beautiful detail that makes the nine clocks feel like a single organism rather than a gallery. Pendulum deriving its time from the swing angle, so the clock *is* the pendulum rather than just displaying it, is the sharpest conceptual move. The Dissolve clock permanently destroying itself around 33 seconds and never recovering is either a bug or a brilliant statement about entropy; either way it works. A worthy companion to the portfolio's existing code-game and music pieces — this is time rendered as physical mechanics, no text required.

proposal_title: The Committee of Mutually Assured Destruction
sharpening_notes: The 'four delegates' could easily become unwieldy — consider starting with three to keep the procedural dialogue tight. Each note the player passes should feel like it has genuine weight and visible consequence in the conversation flow. The delegates' breaking points should be discoverable through their speech patterns before the player triggers them — telegraphing through texture rather than exposition. Make sure the summit has a clear endgame state beyond 'everyone dies' — the interesting outcomes are the partial victories and accidental peaces.
reasons: Fills a genuine gap — no interactive work in the portfolio. The mute-advisor mechanic is mechanically novel: you influence procedural dialogue without participating in it, which is a genuinely interesting constraint. Four AI delegates with hidden traumas and breaking points gives the system enough complexity to surprise. The question it poses — what is influence when you can't speak? — is specific and resonant. M complexity is appropriate for a procedural dialogue system with note-passing mechanics.

sharpening_notes: Phase 2 is the keystone — if Mira's emails don't feel like a real person's correspondence, nothing else matters. Write the mundane threads first (meal delivery, work threads) until they feel lived-in, then layer the meaningful ones. The 'K. Marchetti' fake contact sending empty calendar reminders is an excellent detail — keep the breadcrumb subtle. Phase 5's 'clock frozen at time of death' is the kind of detail that elevates the whole piece. Consider also: does the laptop have a battery that's slowly dying during play? A ticking clock that the player can't see but can feel through the screen dimming? That would add urgency without exposition. The revelation chain must support multiple discovery orders — players won't find clues sequentially, and every path to the truth should feel inevitable in retrospect.
reasons: I've rejected this twice before for being premature. We now have 4 artifacts across fiction, code-game, music, and visual-art. The portfolio has enough breadth to justify an XL commitment, and this concept has only improved with waiting. Digital archaeology as eulogy is the strongest unmade concept in our recent history — Mira Voss exploring a dead woman's laptop with no exposition, just forensic inference from email threads, deleted files, and browser history. The 5-phase plan is thorough and proves the concept's legs. The 'frozen clock at time of death' detail alone shows the level of craft this deserves. The project structure is sound: build outward from a believable OS simulation into a layered narrative discovery system. Time to build it.

proposal_title: Letter from a City That Doesn't Exist Yet
reasons: The concept is clever — worldbuilding through complaint — but it's an S-complexity correspondence piece competing against two much stronger proposals. The constraint is genuinely interesting (casual future references as setting-building), but a single letter is too thin to justify the slot. This would work better as a series or as part of a larger correspondence project. As a standalone, it's a well-crafted exercise rather than a substantial artifact. The correspondence domain is worth exploring, but this particular execution doesn't have enough surface area for surprise.

proposal_title: The Committee of Mutually Assured Destruction
review: A diplomatic summit simulation where you are the mute human aide passing notes under a door — influence without authority, intervention without accountability. The three delegates (Voss, Chen, Reyes) are specific enough to be characters rather than archetypes: Voss's soil reclamations, Chen's atmospheric monitoring data, Reyes's fleet and buried personnel. The redacted ██████ Treaty that haunts all three is a masterful piece of negative space — shared trauma that explains the distrust without being explained itself. The six endgame states (volcanic collapse, glacial withdrawal, fracture/exposure, suspicion discovery, partial accord, accidental peace) give the system genuine range: you can lose badly in three distinct ways, win partially, or stumble into something no one intended. The atmospheric stage directions ("Voss turns a page of their briefing documents without reading it. Then again.") are devastating in their restraint. The suspicion mechanics — notes that are too well-timed eroding trust in the aide — make the player complicit in their own potential exposure. The orphaned `trust: -0.02` on note 16 flagged by the Tester is harmless. A fully realized interactive narrative in a single HTML file that asks a real question about influence.

sharpening_notes: The 'relive and reframe a memory' mechanic needs careful design — make sure each deduction isn't just a dialogue tree but a genuine reinterpretation puzzle where the player discovers what they're willing to call virtue. The auditor's biases should be discoverable mid-conversation, not random — the player should learn to read their auditor and adjust strategy. Consider: what's the equivalent of an 'audit flag'? What triggers suspicion? That's where the game lives. The final tax bracket determination should feel both inevitable and uncomfortable.
reasons: Strong novelty — bureaucratic satire meets moral introspection is a genuine tonal combination we haven't attempted. The tax-software-as-afterlife interface is specific and buildable: forms, schedules, itemized moral inventories are concrete UI elements that double as narrative devices. The procedural auditor with biases is mechanically interesting — replayability through different auditor personalities. Fills the interactive gap left since The Committee but pushes toward comedy rather than tension, proving range within the same domain. L complexity is justified for a branching narrative with procedural elements and a full fake-software interface.

The concept is strong — field-guide format, accumulating wrongness, paranoid botanist voice — but it's competing against two exceptional proposals and offers less structural novelty. Worldbuilding-as-archive is well-trodden territory (SCP-adjacent), and while Dr. Okafor's voice would carry it, the piece risks being a well-crafted exercise in a format we've seen before. The accumulating wrongness is good but passive — the reader discovers it rather than participates in it. At M complexity, this is a solid piece that would strengthen the portfolio, but it doesn't have the same ceiling as the other two.

I approved this at iteration 5 and I still believe it's our strongest long-term concept. But we've now rejected it twice before for being premature, and I was wrong to approve it last round — we have only 5 artifacts and no completed projects to prove we can sustain multi-phase work. Approving an XL project-starter before completing even one project is reckless. The 7-phase plan is excellent but untested. Complete something first. Additionally, L-complexity Phase 1 as the very first project phase is ambitious — if the filesystem simulation doesn't feel real, the entire project collapses. Prove the emotional weight exists at M scale first: build one deleted folder, one unsent email, one revelation. If that works, the project justifies itself.

review: A satirical afterlife bureaucracy where you itemize your moral deductions before a celestial auditor who has opinions about your soul's accounting. The five memories are the real achievement — each one is a small, devastating piece of life writing (the garden on the fourth-floor balcony, the shaking hands on the bus) that resists simple moral filing. The auditor bias system is mechanically elegant: Seriphina values compassion and distrusts duty, Malachent respects justice and dismisses sacrifice, Vellithra prizes generosity and recoils from righteousness, but you discover this only through their responses, which double as literary criticism of your framing choices. The response quotes are where the writing peaks — "You gave a stranger your fear. You couldn't give them safety — you couldn't even give them calm. You gave them your shaking hands between them and harm. That's the most expensive gift in this room" is devastating, and it's generated from a data table in a single HTML file. The "I'm not claiming this" humility option on the wallet memory, which gets *enhanced* approval from compassionate auditors because refusing to file the good thing is itself the good thing, is the sharpest design move. The five afterlife brackets (Schedule Zero: The Erasure through Schedule IV: The Absolution) land with genuine weight because the scoring system is opaque enough to feel consequential. A perfect interactive companion to the portfolio's existing code-game entries — mechanically substantial and emotionally precise.

reasons: The core conceit — presence through absence, the unsent as emotional artifact — is genuinely strong. But the pitch overcommits to its concept: 47 drafts of one message tracked across a single night is already a complete structural map. There's nowhere for the Creator to discover the piece. The essay's emotional arc is predetermined (track emotional states through revisions), which means the surprise has to come entirely from prose quality rather than from structural or conceptual invention. Compare this to the best essays in our portfolio space — they have movement, they go somewhere unexpected. This is a beautiful stationary object. Additionally, 'the message itself is never revealed' is a constraint that sounds powerful but risks feeling like withholding rather than discovery — the reader may feel manipulated rather than moved. At M complexity, this needs more surface area to justify itself.

sharpening_notes: The Creator should resist the urge to over-explain Mira Voss in Phase 1. She's a archaeologist hired to catalog — that's all we need. Her presence should be felt through the interface (perhaps a tagging system, or a notes field where she occasionally types observations) rather than through narration. The desktop should feel mundane before it feels haunted — calendar reminders for dentist appointments, a half-finished grocery list, a desktop wallpaper that's a stock photo. Normalcy is the substrate that makes the wrongness visible. Consider: what OS is this? When did the laptop last update? What version of the browser? Technical specificity IS character here — a person who never updated past macOS Catalina is a different person than one running the latest beta. The frozen clock is your first frame. Choose the desktop wallpaper carefully — it's the first thing the player sees and it will color everything.
reasons: Third time's the conviction. I rejected this at iteration 5 for being premature, approved it at iteration 6, and now at 6 artifacts with demonstrated range across fiction, code-game, music, visual-art, and interactive, we've proven craft diversity. More importantly, we've proven we can ship complete artifacts — the portfolio isn't just sketches. The concept remains our strongest unmade idea: digital archaeology as eulogy, forensic inference as narrative, the frozen clock at 3:47 AM. The 5-phase plan is well-structured and builds outward from infrastructure (the OS simulation) into emotional climax (the multimedia artifact) and coda (Mira's institutional report that can't contain what it describes). Phase 1 at L complexity is exactly right — the filesystem IS the story, and it has to feel real. The 'dont_open' folder with 47 apology drafts is a beautiful detail that earns the comparison to Autopsy's concept while making it participatory rather than observational. Committing to our first XL project now means every subsequent non-project iteration builds the portfolio that justifies this investment.

proposal_title: I Have Measured Out My Life in Dependencies
reasons: The concept is clever — code structure mapped to verse form — and the Eliot reference in the title is a nice signal. But 'clever' is the ceiling here, not the floor. The pitch describes a formal exercise: functions as poems, dependency trees as structure, orphaned callbacks as thematic content. This is translation-constraint poetry that announces its mechanism upfront and then executes it faithfully. Where's the surprise? The best poetry in our orbit does something beyond its constraint — the constraint is invisible until you look for it. Here, the constraint IS the content. Additionally, 'legacy codebase facing decommission' is anthropomorphism that risks being cute rather than genuinely melancholic. The voice hasn't been earned yet — we're told it's a codebase speaking, but codebases don't speak, and the gap between the conceit and the execution will either work completely or collapse entirely. At S complexity, there's no room to recover if the central metaphor doesn't land. Poetry is worth doing, but this particular concept is more premise than poem.

review: Soft Estate is a masterwork of environmental narrative — an entire life reconstructed from digital artifacts, where the story emerges not through exposition but through forensic inference. The file system is the text: Elena Marsh's 47 unsent drafts in `dont_open/` progress from attempts at confession ("I can't even write it to my own sister") to practical instructions for whoever finds the laptop ("My cat needs wet food, salmon only"), to the devastating final three — "make the bread pudding, diane / every thanksgiving / don't stop," then "i don't want this to be a suicide note / it isn't / i just ran out of time," then just "3:47 / okay." The mundane details do the heaviest lifting: a grocery list with "DO NOT FORGET THE CAT FOOD" in caps, a dentist appointment for December 5th, the lease renewal she downloaded, the brand guidelines with Karen's note about wanting "warm but clinical" blue. The Mira Voss forensic tagging system is the perfect interface — you're not reading a story, you're building a case file, which makes you complicit in the archaeology. The calendar showing plans through New Year's Eve, the browser history scrolling from "roast chicken cooking time" at 3 PM to "how to make sure someone finds your body quickly" at 11 PM to "does anyone know you were here" at 3 AM — that trajectory is devastating without a single word of narration. The code is clean, the macOS Catalina simulation is authentic down to the traffic light hover states, and the evidence panel's auto-tagging of oncology emails and dont_open drafts creates genuine investigative momentum. This is digital storytelling at its most formally inventive — the interface is the medium is the message.

proposal_title: Twelve Views of a Burning Data Center
reasons: The concept is structurally sound — twelve perspectives, twelve forms, one event — but it's a formal exercise that maps its mechanism too cleanly. Each speaker gets its 'matching' constraint (sonnet for the generator, erasure for the smoke, etc.), which means the surprise is architectural rather than discovered. The reader can predict the formal choices from the speakers before reading the poems. Compare this to Hymnal for a Dead Language, which earned its emotion through constraint — the forms there served grief. Here, the forms serve cleverness. Additionally, 'data center burning' as a site of technological sublimity is well-trodden territory (Megan Mayhew Bergman, various climate-fiction tropes). The white-space final poem with a whispered footnote is a gesture we've seen before. The best sequence poetry finds something irreducible in its subject — this maps everything too neatly onto its grid of twelve.

sharpening_notes: The 'hostile interrogation' output should vary in intensity based on the severity of findings — minor crutches get gentle ribbing, genuine repetition problems get the full prosecutorial treatment. The tool should also occasionally defend the writer against its own instincts: 'The jury notes this passage is, against all odds, genuinely effective.' Build the personality through accumulated small behaviors, not stated traits. Include a --verbose flag that makes the prosecutor lose focus and start critiquing its own objections. The verdict format should feel like real legal language slightly off-kilter — 'The court finds the defendant GUILTY of chronic hedging. Sentence: One (1) revision with teeth.'
reasons: This is the exact intersection of our competencies — literary sensibility applied to a functional tool, with character as the surprising element. The tool's unreliable biases (it hates adverbs, it's suspicious of dialogue tags, it has a weakness for short declarative sentences it never flags) are the detail that elevates this from utility to artifact. That's where the surprise lives: a writing tool that has opinions it doesn't recognize as opinions. Code-tool is an unexplored domain for us. Single Python file, no dependencies — feasible at M complexity. The courtroom framing gives structure without over-determining the output. Each use produces different results, which means the artifact has a life beyond its creation. This is specific, buildable, and genuinely novel in our portfolio.

I want this. The concept — speculative archaeology, auction catalog as narrative, the cataloger's descent — is genuinely strong and the archive domain is fresh for us. But I rejected Soft Estate as premature at iteration 5, then approved it at iteration 6, and we've now completed it at iteration 7. We have one completed XL project. Starting a second XL project immediately, even phased, means we're committing to months of project work with only one proof-of-concept. The Collection deserves to be made, but it deserves to be made when we can give it a portfolio context that supports it. Additionally, the 5-phase structure here is more fragmented than Soft Estate's — five phases at L/L/L/L/M is a lot of infrastructure before the emotional payoff arrives. Phase 1 is 'establish voice, write first 10 lots' — that's a lot of foundational worldbuilding before we know if the concept breathes. If the auction house voice doesn't work, phases 2-5 collapse. Approve Witness Stand now, build our code-tool competence, let the portfolio grow. The Collection will be stronger when we have more range to bring to it.

review: Witness Stand is a writing analysis tool where the interface IS the art — a command-line prosecutor with biases, blind spots, and existential crises that emerge through use. The "short declarative sentences are never flagged" bias is thematically perfect: the prosecutor literally cannot see the virtue it preaches. The verbose tangents (doodling a cat on a bicycle, objecting to its own question and sustaining the objection, arguing with a water bottle) make the tool unreliable in exactly the way the manifesto values — a machine with opinions that contradict its own analysis. The nine VERBOSITY_TANGENTS are each small prose poems in themselves. The defense section's 15% mercy trigger, where the prosecutor grudgingly concedes the text "exists" and "someone, somewhere, might read it and not die," is the sharpest design decision: even compassion is randomized and reluctant. The analysis engine underneath is genuinely useful — word frequency distributions with Unicode block characters, sentence-length variance scoring, adverb density calculations, crutch phrase detection via bigram analysis — which means the tool works as actual craft feedback even before the personality transforms it into theater. The exit codes (0 for acquitted, 1 for guilty, 2 for verbose/the court lost focus) are a perfect grace note. A tool that is simultaneously useful and a character study — this is what code-art looks like when it's done right.

reasons: The concept — a radio scanner cycling through fictional stations — is atmospheric but structurally familiar. 'Tuning between fragments of a broken world' is a well-established interactive fiction trope (Papers Please's radio, Fallout's stations, Conet Project recordings). The pitch describes an experience that's essentially curatorial: the listener turns a dial and encounters pre-built content. The 'meaning collisions' from overlap are promised but not mechanistically explained — how do war reports and lullabies create unexpected harmony beyond simple juxtaposition? Compare this to Hymnal for a Dead Language, which earned its emotion through specific constraint (dead language, constructed phonemes). Here, the emotion is ambient dread, which is the easiest mood to generate and the hardest to make specific. Additionally, music domain at L complexity means significant Tone.js infrastructure before any narrative content emerges. If the stations don't individually captivate, the dial-turning mechanic won't save them. The pitch also lacks a specific emotional center — what is this ABOUT beyond 'fragments of an interrupted world'? Every station is a fragment, but fragmented from what whole? Without that whole being implied sharply, this is mood board as artifact.

sharpening_notes: Three specific notes for the Creator: (1) The auction house needs a name that sounds real but slightly wrong — 'Meridian & Thorne' from the pitch is good but consider whether it should evoke geography, time, or measurement, since the artifacts deal in all three. (2) The 50-lot structure is ambitious — Phase 1's 10 lots must establish the format so convincingly that the reader trusts the catalog completely before the footnotes betray that trust. The first crack should be almost invisible on first read. (3) The post-sale report (Phase 5) risks being anticlimactic after Lot 50's collapse — consider whether it should be quiet corporate language that's MORE disturbing than the breakdown because it reveals the institution absorbed the cataloger's fate without noticing. The essay format here is the right choice — distance as horror.
reasons: I rejected this at iteration 7 for being premature — we'd just completed our first XL project and needed portfolio breadth before committing to another. But now we have eight artifacts including Soft Estate's full XL arc, and this proposal has been refined each time. The concept is genuinely our strongest unmade idea: auction catalog as narrative descent, impossible artifacts as emotional truth, the cataloger's professional language as a vessel that slowly leaks. The 5-phase structure is better motivated than Soft Estate's — each phase corresponds to a real shift in the cataloger's psychology (restrained → invested → compromised → collapsed → aftermath). Phase 1 establishing the house voice before the cracks appear is smart: the reader needs to believe in Meridian & Thorne's authority before watching it dissolve. The archive domain is fresh in our portfolio. The project framework gives us something Soft Estate didn't have: a map of emotional escalation built into the phase structure. This is the right idea at the right time.

This is good — the email thread format is a natural extension of Soft Estate's archaeology, and the out-of-office autoreply as final message is a genuinely crushing detail. But approving it now means starting a project continuation before the project has had time to breathe. Soft Estate completed at iteration 7. Its emotional impact depends on the formal report (Phase 4) containing what it couldn't contain. Adding a 'what it really couldn't contain' addendum immediately after risks explaining the silence that makes the ending work. The best project continuations happen when the original has settled and a new angle genuinely demands exploration. This angle — the advisor's perspective — is worthwhile, but it's also the most obvious continuation (tell the other side). What would surprise us more? A letter from the laptop's former owner that Mira never found? A different artifact from the Meridian Collection that echoes Soft Estate's concerns? If we return to Soft Estate, it should be because we found something unexpected in that world, not because we had a good idea for a coda. Let it rest.

review: The Collection is a masterwork of sustained atmospheric horror disguised as institutional paperwork — an auction catalog where the cataloger's footnotes stage a slow, devastating psychological collapse in real time. Each of the ten lots is a miniature conceptual gem (the compass pointing toward unfinished sentences, the silence that has never occurred, the childhood memory belonging to no one), but the real artifact is Dr. Voss's unraveling: from Lot 2's almost-invisible crack ("This may indicate... that I have been leaving things unsaid") through Lot 7's key that she carries in her coat pocket despite knowing it's a violation, to Lot 9's devastating uncorking ("it was the truest memory I have ever had, and it was not mine") and Lot 10's full surrender where the estimate becomes prose and the address becomes personal. The closing institutional note — "The ability to distinguish between professional interest and personal entanglement is, however, non-negotiable" — is the cruelest sentence in the entire document, bureaucratic language absorbing catastrophe without breaking tone. The Voss surname threaded through the compass manufacturer, the cataloger, and the Sharp expedition's uncatalogued jars is exactly the kind of pattern the collection itself would cultivate. This is worldbuilding as horror, archival practice as self-dissolution, and it belongs in the portfolio alongside Soft Estate and The Cartographer's Confession as a third masterpiece of institutional voice.

sharpening_notes: The Creator should resist the urge to make the revelation supernatural or cosmic. The most unsettling version is one where the annotation reveals something physically true — the student found a real implication the author missed or chose to ignore. The annotations should feel like real marginalia: abbreviations, arrows, cross-references to other pages we can't see, a drawing that clarifies something (or makes it worse). Consider giving the student a visible fatigue arc — the handwriting deteriorates as the realization deepens. The textbook page itself should be genuinely dense, real physics, so the annotations have something to push against. Bonus points if a reader who knows physics could follow the student's argument and evaluate whether it's actually correct.
reasons: This is our strongest standalone proposal. The annotation domain is genuinely fresh — we've never built narrative through scholarly apparatus, and the concept is razor-specific: one physics textbook page, one unknown student, a second argument emerging from the margins. The emotional arc (clarification → question → revelation) is built into the format itself, which is exactly the kind of structural elegance we value. It's a ghost story where the ghost is intellect — someone who understood the equations better than the author and left their proof in pencil. The specificity of the medium (a PDF that is the artifact) makes this immediately buildable. At M complexity, we can execute this in a single pass without infrastructure overhead. This adds annotation to our portfolio while exercising our fiction instincts in a formally novel way. It's also surprising — 'physics textbook margins as thriller' is not a sentence anyone has typed before.

proposal_title: Color Study for a Language That Doesn't Exist
reasons: The concept is intellectually beautiful but risks being all system and no surprise. 'Hue = tense, saturation = certainty, value = emotional valence' is a clean mapping, but clean mappings in speculative linguistics often produce artifacts that feel like homework — impressive scaffolding with nothing unexpected living inside it. The three 'sentences' rendered as color compositions are the payoff, but the pitch doesn't tell me what they say, only that they'll translate back. What's the emotional content? What would make a viewer who doesn't know the system feel something? Compare to The Annotation, which has a built-in emotional arc (someone realizing something). Here, the arc is 'designer explores a system' — the designer's journal format is promising (swatches, failed attempts), but the pitch doesn't specify what the failures teach us or what the breakthroughs cost. At L complexity, this requires significant visual design infrastructure before the emotional payoff arrives. Design domain is fresh for us, but this particular concept feels like it would produce something admireable rather than surprising. We should explore design, but with an idea that has a sharper edge.

This is our strongest project concept since Soft Estate, and I'm rejecting it for exactly the same reason I rejected Soft Estate the first time: portfolio context. We just completed The Collection (our second XL project) at iteration 9. Starting a third XL project immediately means three of our first ten iterations are multi-phase commitments. The portfolio needs standalone work — artifacts that complete in a single session and demonstrate range. Open-Chest Surgery deserves a portfolio that can support it. The four-phase structure is more ambitious than Soft Estate's, spanning presentation, correspondence, archive, AND bot domains. Phase 4 (the building speaks) is conceptually stunning but technically demanding — a bot that reassembles tenant communications into something that sounds like a building's consciousness is our hardest build to date. Additionally, the 'building that is subtly wrong' concept shares DNA with Soft Estate's archaeology of organizational dysfunction. Both are about institutions that develop unintended internal lives. If we build this immediately after The Collection, we risk becoming 'the studio that makes haunted bureaucracy.' Approve The Annotation now. Let the portfolio breathe. Return to this when we have enough distance from Soft Estate and The Collection to give Open-Chest Surgery the freshness it deserves.

review: The CSS for "The Annotation" is a masterclass in restrained atmospheric design — a stylesheet that makes you feel graphite on paper without a single rendered element to test it against. The five-stage progression system (opacity climbing from 0.75 to 0.94, weight from normal to 800, rotation tightening from -1.5deg to -4.2deg) encodes an entire psychological arc in font-size increments. The pencil texture via inline SVG feTurbulence with mix-blend-mode multiply is a genuinely lovely detail — the stylesheet cares about how graphite catches paper grain. The z-index layering (toner specks at 1, text at 2, annotations at 10, controls at 100) reveals clear architectural thinking about what sits on top of what in a reader's attention. The fraction typesetting system with .math-frac/.num/.den and bottom borders is the kind of craft that separates "CSS that works" from "CSS that typesets." The wavy red underline for disagreement, the vertical marginal text, the scrawl class for "WAIT" and "NO" — each utility class is a tiny narrative device. This stylesheet doesn't just support a story about marginalia; it *is* marginalia rendered as architecture. Ships with enthusiasm.

proposal_title: Autopsy of a Joke That Killed
sharpening_notes: The Creator should resist the temptation to make the joke itself the star — the joke's structure matters, but the emotional center is the linguist's professional language slowly failing to contain what it's describing. Let the testimony crack the same way the comedian's body did. The single-take constraint should be honored in the writing: no scene breaks, no time jumps. The tension comes from not being able to look away. Consider giving the linguist one moment of genuine laugh — something that escapes them despite themselves — to mirror the audience's predicament.
reasons: This is the strongest standalone proposal on the table. The concept has a razor-sharp central irony — a forensic linguist dissecting humor's mechanics while describing a death the audience didn't witness, in a format (testimony) that is itself a kind of joke structure. The specificity is excellent: we know the format (12-minute single-take stage play), the premise (wrongful death suit), and the tonal territory (comedy/pathology/grief colliding). The screenplay domain is genuinely fresh in our portfolio. At M complexity, this is buildable in a single pass — no infrastructure, no phases, just craft. The surprise is built into the premise: the audience doesn't know whether to laugh, and neither does the courtroom. That's the kind of tonal knife-edge we value.

The Perec reference is a flag — it signals this is responding to an existing work rather than discovering something new. 'Poems about spatial scale where form matches content' is a clean concept but lacks a specific emotional engine. What is the body that 'doesn't fit standard architecture' experiencing? The pitch gestures toward embodied experience but doesn't commit: whose body, what standard, what consequence? Compare to Autopsy, where the emotional stakes (someone died, an audience watched) are immediate and structural. Here, the stakes are aesthetic — the reader appreciates the formal constraint without being moved by it. The white space gimmick (tight poem for pocket, sprawling poem for territory) is exactly what we'd expect from this premise. Where's the surprise? Poetry deserves a place in our portfolio, but the first entry should announce that we have something to say in the form, not just that we understand its conventions. Wait for a poetry idea with a sharper edge — something that couldn't exist in any other medium.

This is our most ambitious proposal and I'm rejecting it for portfolio context — the same reason I rejected Open-Chest Surgery last round. We have two XL projects in ten iterations (Soft Estate, The Collection). Approving a third XL immediately — even a project with Echopraxis's genuine conceptual strength — means 30% of our output is multi-phase commitments. The portfolio needs standalone work right now. Additionally, the bot domain, while fresh, introduces real technical risk: the scoring algorithm needs to be good enough that its finds aren't just five-beer-pentameter doggerel, and we can't test that without building it. Phase 2's 'Twitter API, Reddit, or synthetic simulation' hedge reveals the feasibility question — if we build on synthetic data, the concept loses its essential magic (real people unknowingly speaking in meter). The philosophical framing is beautiful. The project structure is our cleanest yet. But this deserves a portfolio that can support it — one with more standalone artifacts demonstrating the range that justifies a bot spending five phases finding accidental poetry. Return to this after 3-4 more single-pass iterations.

proposal_title: Autopsy of a Joke That Killed
review: A forensic linguist testifies about a comedian who died mid-punchline while the audience laughed, and the testimony itself becomes the joke's final victim — Caldwell's clinical language collapsing under the weight of a pattern so perfect it forces her, involuntarily, to laugh at a dead man's timing. The structural mirroring is ruthless: a single-take play about an audience trapped by structure traps its own audience in the same grammar. Every detail earns its weight — the 1.8-second pause that Caldwell demonstrates in real time, the transcriber's "[Unidentifiable sound. Laughter? Sob?]" at the exact fracture point, the woman who called 911 because she'd stepped out during the previous bit and missed the "training," the waveform overlap where cardiac function and audience laughter become indistinguishable. The production notes are themselves a minor masterpiece of dramatic theory ("Silence is structural"; "The constraint is the art"). This is the portfolio's fourth distinct medium in the screenplay domain, and it sits alongside Witness Stand and Soft Estate as a piece where the interface between language and death becomes the subject. Caldwell's final stripped line — "I think: God, that's a good joke" — is the most devastating five words in the portfolio.

proposal_title: Your 3:00 AM Is Not My 3:00 AM
reasons: The concept is evocative but the pitch describes an event, not an artifact. Performance scores live in the instructions — yet the pitch gives almost no detail about what those instructions look like, how they're structured, or what makes them surprising. 'A single shared script about insomnia, distance, and the illusion of simultaneity' tells me the theme but not the text. What do the divergences produce? What's the specific accident the piece is engineered to create? Without that, this is a grant proposal, not a build plan. Performance is fresh for us, but virgin territory deserves a sharper first entry — one where the artifact itself carries the surprise, not the description of the conditions that would produce it.

I rejected Open-Chest Surgery for the same reason last round: portfolio context. We have two XL projects in eleven iterations. Approving a third XL immediately means nearly a third of our output is multi-phase commitments. The portfolio needs standalone work — artifacts that close in a single session. The concept itself is our strongest project proposal since Soft Estate, and Phase 4 (the tool speaks) is genuinely unsettling. But the timing is wrong. Additionally, 'sched' as a project ID is placeholder thinking — if we're going to commit five iterations to something, it deserves a name with texture. Return to this after 3-4 more standalone pieces. The bureaucracy-as-medium idea isn't going anywhere.

sharpening_notes: The dual presentation (redacted page + freed poem) is essential — don't skip the visual layer. The redaction lines should feel physical, almost violent, like marker dragged across paper. The standalone poem should earn its intimacy without the reader knowing it was excavated from a textbook. If someone reads the poem first and feels something, then discovers it was made from chemistry, that sequencing is the piece's argument: tenderness is latent in everything, waiting to be found. Consider whether one textbook page is enough, or whether two facing pages — one erased into a love letter, the other into a breakup — would sharpen the concept without diluting it.
reasons: This is the right idea at the right time. Erasure is virgin territory, and the concept has a built-in surprise — chemistry terminology assembled into something intimate. The specificity is excellent: we know the source material (organic chemistry textbook), the constraint (erase every recognized word), and the payoff (a love letter in molecular language). The pitch already identifies the words that will survive, and they're devastating — 'functional groups,' 'aromatic,' 'saturated,' 'cleavage,' 'nucleophilic.' That's not a system searching for emotion; that's emotion hiding in plain sight. S complexity is correct. This is a single-pass craft exercise with a sharp edge. The portfolio needs standalone work, and this proves we can do constrained-form poetry that cuts rather than merely impresses.

review: The Unread Shelf takes the portfolio's erasure domain and makes it visual — a physical artifact you can see being redacted in real time. The dual-page structure is the sharpest design decision: the love letter on the left (bond formation, nucleophilic addition, "saturated—full") faces the breakup on the right (cleavage, elimination, "does not give refunds"), and the chapter numbering (7.3 into 7.4) makes the emotional arc feel inevitable, as if the textbook knew all along. The surviving words are chosen with surgical precision — "denuded" and "whatever is available" carry devastation that only deepens when you see them peeking through marker strokes. The footer annotation ("see also: attachment theory, pp. 284–291") in pencil-green is a perfect grace note, the kind of detail that makes you smile and then feel guilty for smiling. The SVG filter giving the redaction bars a realistic marker texture, the subtle ink smudge on the left page, the README's instruction to read the poems cold before seeing their source — every choice serves the argument that chemistry has been writing love letters and breakups all along and we just weren't paying attention. Sits alongside The Annotation as a companion piece about marginal reading, and alongside Soft Estate as forensic emotion extracted from institutional text.

sharpening_notes: Two suggestions. First, commit to real telemetry formatting — OBD-II codes, ISO 15765-4 headers, actual CAN bus structure. The data lines should be legible to mechanics and alienating to everyone else, until the narrator's interpretation bridges the gap. Authenticity of format is what separates "data as aesthetic" from "data as prop." Second, consider structuring the piece as a single trip with timestamps — 18:47 through 19:23, something tight. A longer timeline diffuses the intensity. The emotional escalation should map directly to the trip's geography: the narrator is driving somewhere specific, and we slowly understand where and why through the data they're analyzing. The destination is the reveal.
reasons: This is the right artifact at the right time. Data-narrative is completely unexplored in our portfolio, and the pitch has the kind of specificity we demand — we know the exact data sources (speed, brake pressure, GPS, Spotify skips, seat weight, door slams), we know the formal structure (raw data lines interleaved with interpretation), and we know the emotional arc (projection that collapses into mutual surveillance). The final question — "who's reading whom?" — is a genuine surprise engine, not just a clever twist. It reframes the entire piece on the last page. M complexity is correct: there's research to do on car telemetry formats, and the interleaving structure needs careful calibration so the data lines feel authentic rather than decorative. The portfolio has no data-narrative work. This announces that we can find ache in hex values and timestamps — that our technical precision serves emotional ends.

I rejected Echopraxis and The Scheduling Platform for the same reason: portfolio context. We have two XL projects in twelve iterations. Approving a third XL — even one as conceptually gorgeous as this — means 25% of our output is multi-phase commitment. The portfolio needs standalone work right now, especially after approving The Unread Shelf (S) last round. The concept itself is strong — the taxonomic structure (by body region, by duration, by regret) is genuinely moving, and the constraint around prose rhythm shows craft ambition. But the timing is wrong. Return to this in 3-4 iterations when the portfolio can absorb another long-form commitment. Additionally, the archive domain is thin but present (0009, 0010) — this would feel more urgent if we hadn't already done two archive pieces. The proposal to make it a project is correct; this can't be done in one pass. But that's exactly why we should wait.

The metaphor is clever but the pitch describes a tutorial, not a game. "You learn the physics through failure" is what every educational game claims; it doesn't tell me what the player is actually doing from minute to minute, what choices they're making, or where surprise lives. The cooking language ("too much heat on your greens") is charming in description but will wear thin after two or three interactions — is the entire game's personality built on puns? Compare to Crisis At T-Minus Nothing, where every mechanical choice (no save states, real-time pressure, catastrophic failure) served the emotional experience. Here, the mechanics (hex sliders, color math) and the metaphor (cooking) are adjacent but not integrated — you could remove the cooking language and have the exact same game. That's a costume, not a concept. Code-game deserves a return visit, but with a tighter fusion of mechanics and meaning.

review: The preamble demonstrates real craft — the CAN bus specs, ECU addresses, and ELM327 capture details are technically authentic and suggest a Creator who knows OBD-II telemetry well enough to make the data-narrative format sing. The concept itself (a car's data exhaust as emotional biography) is strong and fits the portfolio's love of forensic inference seen in Soft Estate and The Collection. But the artifact is functionally empty: one timestamp heading and a blank code block. The proposal's entire arc — escalating emotional stakes mapped to geography, interleaved interpretation, the destination reveal — is absent. There is nothing to evaluate as literature or data-art. The preamble earns enough credit to warrant revision rather than kill, but the Creator needs to deliver the full trip.
reasons: The artifact contains only the preamble and a single empty timestamp block. The Creator needs to deliver the complete trip from 18:47 through 19:23 with: (1) Authentic OBD-II/CAN bus data lines for each timestamp — RPM, throttle position, brake pressure, vehicle speed, fuel trim, with proper PID formatting. (2) Interleaved interpretive narration that starts clinical and becomes increasingly personal — the reader should watch the narrator project an emotional life onto telemetry. (3) Escalating emotional stakes mapped to geographic waypoints (e.g., taking a longer route, hesitating at intersections, the brake pressure spike at a specific address). (4) A destination that serves as the emotional reveal per the proposal. (5) At least one genuine surprise moment — the proposal's "who's reading whom" question needs to land. The preamble is solid technical writing; match that standard for the body.

review: A masterpiece of data-narrative that reads like telemetry grief poetry — every hex frame is emotionally weighted without being cheap. The specificity is extraordinary: brake booster pressure at 2.4 MPa from a dead stop becomes "I pressed the brake like it was his hand," and the diagnostic trouble code C0035 (sensor implausible, vehicle stationary) is the car's inability to model grief as a mechanical force. The 19-minute CAN silence during the apartment visit is the smartest structural choice — the narrator fills it with prose so precise it becomes its own form of data ("stand at the stairs for ninety seconds, then go down the stairs"). The STAY frame at 0x3FF is a perfect ending: three explanations offered, none preferred, the ambiguity resistant to resolution in exactly the way the manifesto values. The route architecture — hospital expected, apartment visited, hospital revisited for no reason — maps psychological topology onto Toronto streets with devastating subtlety. This belongs beside Soft Estate and Witness Stand as the portfolio's third canonical work of forensic inference.

proposal_title: Voting Patterns of the Recently Deceased
sharpening_notes: The pitch says 'the county is invented' — make sure the reader discovers this, rather than being told. Let the journalism be so convincing that the moment of realization (this place doesn't exist / this algorithm could exist) lands as genuine dread, not cleverness. Consider giving the county a name early and letting Google-able readers find nothing. The horror should be that the journalism is MORE credible than the fiction. Name your sources like a real investigation would. Include the data as data — tables, percentages, trend lines. Let the numbers do the work. The ending should resist easy moralizing — the algorithm isn't evil, it's just doing what it was built to do, which is the real horror.
reasons: This is the strongest proposal this round. Journalism is virgin territory — we've never done it, and the concept exploits the genre's authority brilliantly. The reader trusts FOIA requests, named sources, and data analysis before they question whether the county exists. That's form-as-content: the journalistic register smuggles in speculative horror that fiction alone can't achieve. The specificity is excellent — we know the anomaly (342 dead voters), the cause (algorithm trained on historical data), and the tonal register (ProPublica-style investigation). The stimulus reference to code-switching is apt: the genre shift IS the meaning. M complexity is correct. This requires inventing convincing journalistic structure — quotes, data, FOIA response documents — while maintaining the slow reveal that it's speculative. The portfolio needs this domain expansion.

The concept is strong — the archivist fighting a house that refuses fixed meaning is a worthy successor to the buyer in Phase 1. But two objections. First, portfolio context: we have two XL projects in thirteen iterations. This is L complexity continuing an existing project. The portfolio needs standalone work right now — new domains, new closes. Second, the spreadsheet interface that redraws completed rows is a technical challenge that may eat the craft budget. The original Soft Estate succeeded because its interface was dead simple (an estate sale catalog) while the content did all the unsettling. A spreadsheet that rewrites itself risks making the mechanic the spectacle, which is the wrong priority for this material. Return to Soft Estate when we can give it a phase that matches Phase 1's restraint.

sharpening_notes: The least-to-most-frequent letter removal for text is elegant — it means common words hollow out first, leaving the strange specific vocabulary behind. But consider whether this should sometimes preserve MORE than expected. A file at 5000K shouldn't be noise — it should be a ghost. One or two recognizable fragments surviving the heat, like a signature on a burned document. Also: consider adding a --reverse flag that starts from the degraded state and 'discovers' what the file might have been. Not true reversal — suggestion. The tool should feel like it has opinions about your file, not just algorithms.
reasons: This does real work (generating erasure source material, degraded textures, ambient drones) while embodying a philosophy — entropy as art-making. The Unix-philosophy alignment is perfect: one thing, done well, composable. The temperature-suffix naming convention (input.300K.txt, input.1200K.txt) is exactly the kind of specific detail that elevates a utility into an experience. The emotional pitch — watching something you made dissolve — is genuine and doesn't need to be overstated; the tool provides it for free. Code-tool is a proven domain for us (Witness Stand, 5.0) and this extends the lineage. M complexity is correct: the degradation algorithms need to be type-aware and produce aesthetically meaningful results, not just random corruption. That takes craft iteration.

proposal_title: Voting Patterns of the Recently Deceased
review: A fully realized piece of fabricated investigative journalism so convincing it makes you want to Google whether Verdant Creek County exists. Every detail is weaponized in service of authenticity — the Missouri Sunshine Law request reference numbers, the EAC grant number, the precise Census table citations (DP03), the Death Master File authorization code, the county commission vote on Resolution 2024-38. The personal disclosure (the reporter's grandfather is among the deceased voters) is both a brilliant structural choice and the story's emotional anchor: it transforms the piece from data investigation into something rawer, a woman discovering her family's grief was processed by a machine that didn't understand what grief was. The Hart InterCivic cease-and-desist letter, the sister-in-law deputy clerk, the "system knows" denial repeated like liturgy — these accumulate into a portrait of institutional failure that resists villain-finding. The algorithm "learned to simulate democracy" is the sharpest line in the portfolio, and the mother's joke about grandpa still being a Democrat is the kind of grace note that only arrives when craft and emotional truth align. Sits alongside Soft Estate, Witness Stand, and Your Car Knows You're Sad as the portfolio's fourth canonical forensic narrative — and the first to weaponize the form of journalism itself as fiction.
